question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not verify that the signatures collection contains signatures for distinct nodes, allowing a single compromised node to submit many VERIFIED signatures with the same fileHashAsHex, accumulating enough stake to pass canReachConsensus() and authorize a transaction without approval from other owners?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where the VERIFIED signatures have a fileHashAsHex corresponding to a malicious transaction file, while the majority of signatures (with DOWNLOADED status) correspond to the legitimate file, causing validate() to authorize the malicious file because only VERIFIED signatures are checked?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum collection size check in validate() so that a collection with exactly one VERIFIED signature from a high-stake node passes consensus, authorizing a transaction stream that was only approved by a single signer?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the node stake data in the underlying ConsensusNode objects (e.g., via a stale or poisoned NodeStakeRepository cache) so that getTotalStake() returns an outdated low value, causing getStakeRequiredForConsensus() to compute a threshold that is easily met by a minority of current nodes, authorizing transactions without genuine current-epoch signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not cross-check the totalStake value from the first node against totalStake values from other nodes, so that if nodes report inconsistent totalStake values, the consensus threshold is based on a potentially fraudulent value from the first node only?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where all signatures have status DOWNLOADED (not VERIFIED), causing signatureHashMap to remain empty, and then exploit the consensusRatio == ZERO bypass (line 54) which requires signatureHashMap to be non-empty, so the bypass is not triggered and a SignatureVerificationException is thrown—but could this be chained with another vector to force a false-positive return?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the consensusRatio == ZERO bypass (line 54) returns without setting any signature status to CONSENSUS_REACHED, so downstream code that checks for CONSENSUS_REACHED status may behave differently than expected, potentially treating all signatures as unverified and causing incorrect authorization decisions?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a very large signatures collection (e.g., millions of entries) to cause excessive memory allocation in HashMultimap.create() and the nested iteration loops, resulting in an out-of-memory condition that crashes the importer and prevents legitimate transaction stream authorization (DoS)?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection with a very large number of distinct fileHashAsHex values, causing the outer loop over signatureHashMap.keySet() to iterate over many groups, and if each group's stake is just below the threshold, consensusCount remains 0 and a legitimate transaction is blocked (DoS)?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() mutates the status of input StreamFileSignature objects (line 72) without synchronization, so that in a multi-threaded environment, a concurrent call to validate() with the same signature objects could observe partially-updated statuses, leading to incorrect consensus decisions that authorize transactions without proper signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a fileHash byte array that produces a hex string collision with a legitimate node's fileHashAsHex, causing the attacker's signature to be grouped with legitimate VERIFIED signatures in signatureHashMap, and the combined stake of the group satisfies canReachConsensus(), authorizing a transaction with the attacker's hash?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not validate that the filename is consistent across all signatures in the collection, so that signatures for different files are mixed together, and the combined stake of signatures for a malicious file meets the threshold, authorizing the wrong transaction stream?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where the first signature has a null filename (getFilename() returns null), causing filename to remain null throughout validate(), and the error message in the thrown SignatureVerificationException to contain 'null', potentially leaking internal state or causing downstream null-pointer issues?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the stake accumulation logic where stake is a primitive long (line 64) and node stakes are also longs, so that supplying nodes with negative getStake() values causes the accumulated stake to decrease, potentially making canReachConsensus() return false for a legitimate consensus group and blocking transaction authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where one hash group has exactly the minimum stake to pass canReachConsensus() but represents a forged/replayed transaction file, while the legitimate file's hash group has slightly less stake, causing the forged file to be authorized instead of the legitimate one?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not check whether the VERIFIED signatures were verified against the correct public keys for their respective nodes, so that if the upstream NodeSignatureVerifier is bypassed or misconfigured, signatures with VERIFIED status but invalid cryptographic proofs are accepted by validate(), authorizing transactions without genuine owner signatures?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() trusts the SignatureStatus.VERIFIED flag on input signatures without re-verifying the cryptographic signature, so that if an attacker can set the status field of a StreamFileSignature to VERIFIED (e.g., via deserialization or reflection), the signature is counted toward consensus without any actual cryptographic verification?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where all nodes report the same nodeId but different stake values, causing the stake accumulation to count the same logical node multiple times, inflating the computed stake and satisfying canReachConsensus() without genuine multi-owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the consensusRatio is read from commonDownloaderProperties at runtime (line 98) without caching, so that if the properties are modified concurrently (e.g., via a Spring actuator endpoint), the consensusRatio used in getStakeRequiredForConsensus() differs from the one checked in the ZERO bypass (line 54), creating a TOCTOU window that could allow consensus to be bypassed?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where the VERIFIED signatures all have the same fileHashAsHex as a previously-authorized (replayed) transaction, causing validate() to mark them as CONSENSUS_REACHED and authorize a replayed transaction stream without the current signers' approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not enforce a minimum number of distinct nodes in the consensus group, so that a single node with stake >= stakeRequiredForConsensus can unilaterally authorize a transaction stream, violating the distributed multi-owner approval requirement?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a signatures collection where the totalStake reported by the first node is much smaller than the actual network total stake, causing getStakeRequiredForConsensus() to compute a low threshold, so that a small coalition of nodes (below the true 1/3 threshold) can authorize a transaction without the required owner approval?
